title: Writing Technical Visions That Get Buy-In
description: How Staff+ engineers write compelling technical vision documents that align 5+ teams, secure leadership buy-in, and set technical direction for months to years. Covers the one-page vision template, the 'problem → principles → strategy → roadmap' structure, stakeholder mapping, socializing drafts, handling pushback, and real SAP vision examples rewritten for FAANG context.

description.value:
A technical vision is not a design doc. A design doc describes how to build something. A vision describes where we are going and why it matters. The one-page vision template forces clarity: if you can't explain your vision on one page, you don't understand it well enough.

The one-page vision structure: (1) Problem statement (2-3 sentences): What is wrong with the current state? Quantify the pain. 'Our current payment processing takes 800ms P99, causing a 15% cart abandonment rate. This costs us $10M/year in lost revenue.' (2) Vision statement (1 sentence): What will the future state look like? 'All payments process in under 100ms P99 with 99.99% success rate, enabling real-time checkout across all platforms.' (3) Guiding principles (3-5 bullets): What constraints will guide the design? 'Simplicity over features. Off-the-shelf over custom. Observability by default. Backward compatibility for 18 months.' (4) Strategy (3-5 bullets): What is the approach? 'Phase 1: Instrument and measure current payment flows. Phase 2: Replace the legacy payment gateway with a modern API. Phase 3: Implement retry with idempotency and circuit breakers. Phase 4: Migrate to async payment processing for non-critical flows.' (5) Risks and mitigations: What could go wrong? 'Migration risk: switching payment gateways mid-transaction. Mitigation: dual-write to both gateways for 2 weeks.' (6) Success criteria: How do we know we succeeded? 'P99 < 100ms, error rate < 0.01%, zero P0 incidents for 3 months.'

SAP to FAANG translation: SAP technical designs are often described in SAP-specific terms (RFCs, BAPIs, IDocs, LUWs). A vision document at FAANG must use platform-agnostic language. The Staff+ engineer translates: 'Our current SAP integration uses synchronous RFC calls with 30-second timeouts. The vision is an event-driven architecture using Kafka, with idempotent consumers and dead-letter queues. The SAP integration becomes a Kafka producer — no synchronous coupling.' This translation is critical: the vision must be understandable to engineers who don't know SAP.

A vision document is not written in isolation. It is socialized with stakeholders before, during, and after writing. The goal is to surface objections early and incorporate feedback so the final document has no surprises.

Stakeholder mapping: Before writing, identify: (1) Decision-makers — the VP/Director who must approve the vision. What are their priorities? What questions will they ask? (2) Influencers — senior engineers whose opinions carry weight. Get their buy-in early. (3) Implementers — the teams that will execute the vision. Do they agree with the direction? Do they have concerns about feasibility? (4) Blockers — teams that could block the vision if not aligned. Schedule 1:1s with them before the vision is formalized.

The socializing process: (1) Draft 0 — share with 1-2 trusted senior engineers for brutal feedback. Fix logical gaps before anyone else sees it. (2) Draft 1 — share with all stakeholders as a Google Doc with comments enabled. Give them 1 week. Respond to every comment within 24 hours. (3) Draft 2 — incorporate all feedback. Present in the engineering review forum (weekly design review, architecture review board). (4) Final — share with decision-makers for approval. By this point, there should be no surprises — everyone has been heard and their input has been incorporated.

The 'pre-buttal' technique: If you know a stakeholder will object to something (e.g., 'This will take too long'), address it in the document before they raise it. 'I expect the concern that this migration will take 6 months. Here is why the phased approach reduces risk: each phase delivers independent value and can be rolled back independently. The first phase alone (instrumentation) reduces MTTR by 40% regardless of whether subsequent phases are completed.'

Guiding principles are the most underrated section of a vision document. They serve as a decision filter: when a tradeoff arises during implementation, the team refers to the principles rather than escalating to the vision author. Well-written principles make the vision self-executing.

Characteristics of good guiding principles: (1) They are opinionated. 'We prioritize simplicity over features' is a real tradeoff. It means saying no to feature requests that add complexity. (2) They are actionable. 'Observability by default' means every service must export metrics, logs, and traces before it can be deployed to production. This is a specific, measurable requirement. (3) They conflict with each other. If all principles are perfectly aligned, they are not useful — they don't help with tradeoffs. Good principles create productive tension: 'Ship fast' vs 'Ship with quality.' The team must decide which principle takes priority in each situation. (4) They are few (3-5). More than 5 principles and no one remembers them.

Example principles from FAANG vision documents: (1) 'We build for the long term. Quick fixes that add technical debt require a documented repayment plan.' (2) 'We optimize for the average case, not the edge case. Edge cases get fallback paths, not custom architecture.' (3) 'We own our dependencies. If we rely on an external system, we must understand its failure modes and have mitigation plans.' (4) 'We ship incrementally. No phase should take longer than 6 weeks. Each phase must deliver independent value.' (5) 'We document decisions. Every architecture choice includes the alternatives considered and the rationale for the chosen approach.'

Pushback is a sign that people care about the vision. No pushback means either: (a) everyone genuinely agrees (rare), or (b) no one has read the document carefully enough to find the problems (common). The Staff+ engineer welcomes pushback and uses it to strengthen the vision.

Common objections and how to handle them: (1) 'This is too ambitious.' — 'I agree this is a multi-quarter effort. That is why the vision is broken into independent phases. Phase 1 alone delivers value in 6 weeks. The full vision is the North Star, not the immediate commitment.' (2) 'This is not ambitious enough.' — 'I deliberately scoped this to what we can realistically achieve given our current team size and dependencies. If we find we are moving faster than expected, we can expand the scope in a future revision of the vision.' (3) 'We tried this before and it failed.' — 'I reviewed the previous attempt. Here is what was different: the technology was not mature, the team did not have organizational support, the timeline was too aggressive. Here is how this attempt addresses those specific failure modes.' (4) 'This conflicts with Team X's vision.' — 'I have scheduled a joint meeting with Team X to align our visions. I believe there is a way to reconcile both approaches. Here is my proposed reconciliation.'

The 'yes, and' technique: Instead of defending against objections, incorporate them. 'Yes, this is ambitious, and we can start with Phase 1 to validate the approach.' 'Yes, we tried this before, and the lessons from that attempt inform our new approach.' 'Yes, there is a conflict with Team X's vision, and I have started the alignment conversation.' This shows you are listening and adapting, not defending a fixed position.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "This vision is too abstract. I need concrete timelines and resource commitments. How many engineers, which sprints, what milestones?"
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "I can't give you exact numbers yet — it depends on many factors." (sounds unprepared)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "You are right that the vision document is intentionally high-level. That is because a vision sets the direction, and the roadmap sets the timeline and resources. Here is what I can commit to: (1) Phase 1 (instrumentation) requires 2 engineers for 6 weeks. I already have commitment from my team. (2) Phase 2 (gateway replacement) requires 3 engineers for 8 weeks. I need approval to backfill one position. (3) Phase 3+ timelines depend on Phase 1 and 2 learnings. I will provide updated estimates after Phase 1 completes. The vision is the destination. The roadmap is the route. I'm asking for alignment on the destination today. We will finalize the route in the next planning cycle."
adversarialResponse.pattern: vision-vs-roadmap — distinguish between the destination (vision) and the route (roadmap). The vision sets direction and principles. The roadmap provides timelines and resources. Both are needed, but they serve different purposes and have different levels of certainty.

sections.title: From SAP to FAANG: A Vision Rewrite Case Study

description.value:
Before (SAP-style vision): 'We will implement SAP S/4HANA Cloud with best practices, leveraging the Clean Core approach. The integration will use SAP BTP with Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) to connect to the existing on-premise systems through RFC and IDoc interfaces. We will maintain backward compatibility through a coexistence period of 6 months. The project will follow the SAP Activate methodology with Agile sprint delivery. Key success criteria: go-live within 8 months with zero critical incidents.'

After (FAANG-style vision): 'We will build a real-time integration platform that connects our cloud services to enterprise backends. The platform will abstract the underlying ERP system so that service teams can interact through RESTful APIs and gRPC without knowing whether the backend is SAP, Oracle, or a custom mainframe. Key capabilities: (1) Event-driven synchronization using Kafka — changes in the ERP are published as events and consumed asynchronously. (2) Idempotent API gateway — all write operations are idempotent, enabling safe retries and preventing duplicate records. (3) Circuit breakers and bulkheads — failures in the ERP don't cascade to cloud services. (4) Observability by default — every integration path exports latency, error rate, and throughput metrics. Migration approach: strangler fig pattern — new integrations use the new platform; existing integrations are migrated incrementally over 12 months. Each migration phase is independently rollbackable.'

What changed: (1) Problem-first, not solution-first. The FAANG version starts with the problem (connecting cloud services to backends) rather than the solution (SAP S/4HANA Cloud). (2) Platform-agnostic language. 'Event-driven synchronization' is universally understood; 'RFC and IDoc interfaces' only makes sense to SAP engineers. (3) Modern architecture patterns. Kafka, gRPC, circuit breakers, strangler fig — these are recognizable to any FAANG engineer. (4) Independent value per phase. Each phase is independently valuable and rollbackable. (5) Quantified success criteria. Specific metrics and timelines that can be measured.

sections.title: Vision, Strategy, and Roadmap — The Hierarchy

description.value:
A common mistake is conflating vision, strategy, and roadmap. They serve different purposes, have different audiences, and require different levels of detail. The Staff+ engineer uses all three to set direction at different levels of abstraction.

Vision (1 page, 6-12 month horizon): Sets the destination. Answers: 'Where are we going and why?' Audience: the entire organization including leadership. Key content: problem statement, vision statement, guiding principles. Not: specific timelines, resource allocations, implementation details. Changed: rarely (once or twice per year).

Strategy (2-3 pages, 3-6 month horizon): Sets the approach. Answers: 'How will we get there?' Audience: engineering teams and stakeholders. Key content: phases, architectural decisions, key tradeoffs, risk mitigations. Changed: quarterly (or when new information changes the approach).

Roadmap (living document, sprint-to-quarter horizon): Sets the schedule. Answers: 'What are we doing now, next, and later?' Audience: the execution team and project management. Key content: specific projects, timelines, resource allocations, dependencies, milestones. Changed: every sprint or every month.

The Staff+ skill: The Staff+ engineer holds all three levels simultaneously in their head and can articulate the right level for the right audience. When talking to a VP, they describe the vision. When talking to their team, they describe the roadmap. When talking to peer teams, they describe the strategy. Being able to move between levels fluidly is a hallmark of Staff+ thinking.

details: FAANG Interview Tip: Technical vision writing is a Staff+ interview topic. The question 'Describe a technical vision you set for your organization' tests: (1) Can you think at the organizational level (not just team-level)? (2) Can you communicate a vision clearly and concisely? (3) Can you navigate organizational politics to get buy-in? Use the one-page vision structure in your answer. Describe the problem, the vision, the guiding principles, the strategy, and the stakeholder alignment process. If you have an SAP background, show that you can translate SAP concepts into platform-agnostic language — this demonstrates the abstraction ability expected at Staff+ level.
